dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 4, city of Richmond, a report of a fall with unconscious patient.
Hi, I'm Jason and this is my partner Emily. We are EMTs from Rescue Squad 4. I understand it was a fall. Can you tell us what's going on?
Yes. The patient is a 59-year-old male who fell from his wheelchair. He was being helped up a ramp when he suddenly lost balance, fell, and then didn't wake up.
Does he respond to any stimuli? Do you try talking to him, tapping him gently?
No. We've tried that a few times, but he didn't move and he's not opening his eyes.
Alright. Based on this, the patient is unresponsive. We're going to follow a spinal protocol because of the fall mechanism and unresponsiveness.
Airway is not compromised at this time - patent and maintainable without obstruction.
I'm observing his breathing rate and depth, but due to unresponsiveness, I cannot determine chest rise or symmetry.
Respiratory rate is 14 per minute.
Pulse oximetry shows 89%. His oxygen saturation is low at this time.
Pulse is detected at 82 beats per minute and regular.
Blood pressure is 151/130 mmHg. That is elevated.
Glucose level is 77 mg/dL. This is within normal range.
EKG is showing sinus rhythm.
Peripheral pulses are intact, but I cannot assess skin temperature or color at this time due to the patient being unresponsive.
You mentioned the patient fell from a wheelchair. Can you tell me if he had a history of diabetes or other medical conditions?
Yes. He has insulin-dependent diabetes. I don't know anything else about his past history.
I see. Could you share his name for documentation purposes? Are there any known allergies we should be aware of?
His name is Mr. Thompson. I don't know if he has allergies. He's lived alone recently.
Since he's unresponsive, I'm skipping the pain assessment as it can't be performed right now.
Eyes are unreactive. Pupils are equal and bilateral, measured at 3mm.
No signs of trauma to the head or neck on visual inspection. GCS assessment indicates a low score of 3 (1 for eye, 1 for verbal, 1 for motor), indicating altered mental status.
MS - full spinal restriction of motion is in place. IV access is established in the left arm using 18G.
4-lead EKG monitoring is ongoing for cardiac assessment.
12-lead EKG has been obtained for further interpretation and documentation.
Based on his altered mental status and a history of diabetes, we are following protocol 3-15 for medical emergencies.
Let me recheck the patient's vitals for trending and placement safety.
Pulse remains at 82 beats per minute and regular.
Respiratory rate is now 15 per minute.
Blood pressure is 135/80 now and glucose is 0.
Pulse ox is at 99% now.
EKG continues to show sinus rhythm.
Based on his history and current condition, we are transporting to VCU Health Systems, a trauma center with a hot-line.
We are securing the patient for transport now. Backboard and stretcher are in place.
Let's take the final set of vitals before we load.
Pulse is now at 138 beats per minute.
Respiratory rate is 19 per minute. Blood pressure is 125/67 and glucose is 108.
Pulse oximetry is 99%. EKG still in sinus rhythm.
Patient is ready to be loaded onto the stretcher.
Patient is safely secured and the stretcher has been fully fastened.
This is Jason from Rescue Squad 4. Handing over Mr. Thompson, a 59-year-old male with a fall and unconsciousness call. GCS is at 3.
Vital signs: BLOOD PRESSURE initially at 151/130, now at 125/67. Pulse elevated from 82 to 138. Oximetry improved to 99%. Glucose initially unreadable, now at 108.
Medical history includes insulin-dependent diabetes. No allergies or medications are known.
Transport destination is VCU Health Systems, a trauma center. IV is in place and spinal precautions underway.
No pain interventions were given as the patient is unresponsive.
The patient is now en route to VCU under spinal precautions and continuous monitoring.
The patient was found unresponsive following a fall from his wheelchair. No subjective pain reported.
He is classified as 'unresponsive' per AVPU.
No motor response on stimulus or command.
No eye opening upon examination.
No verbal response observed after multiple verbal attempts.
No gross signs of bruising, petechiae, or rashes at this time. Skin color appears normal.
No palpable lymphadenopathy or observable neck masses.
No thoracic or lung abnormalities evident.
Abdomen flat with no tenderness or palpable masses noted.
No unsupinated extremities, visible deformities, or vertebral instability visible.
No rectal bleeding or urinary abnormalities found.
No immediate signs of airway obstruction or nasal bleeding.
Checking breathing rate again. Breathing is not described as shallow in EPCR but may be shallowness due to unresponsiveness.
No medications have been administered in the field at this point.
IV remains open and on standby. Awaiting final transport confirmation.
We will continue following protocol 3-15 for the en route phase.
No additional interventions were performed beyond spinal immobilization.
No pain assessment was conducted as patient is unresponsive.
No additional information was obtained. Continued documentation on altered mental status and diabetes history.
Transport destination is VCU Health Systems confirmed.
All restraints and equipment are in place for transport.
Final blood pressure reading before transport: 125/67.
Respiratory rate remains at 19 breaths per minute.
Pulse ox is still at 99%.
Final pulse is at 138 beats per minute.
Glucose level is 108 mg/dL.
Patient is now ready for transport and secured.
Transport is en route to VCU Health Systems as planned.
Vital signs current and ready for transport.
Patient and equipment secured and transport en route.
Transport is proceeding under protocol 3-15 and ongoing monitoring is in place.
Spinal precautions and current status will be communicated upon arrival.
Monitoring systems are active and stable.
Final vitals will be conveyed upon arrival and confirming stability.
GCS remains 3. No airway or breathing compromise noted.
Transport is stable with no active interventions.
12-lead EKG and IV are in place and functioning.
Clinical decision made to transport patient to VCU for trauma evaluation.
Equipment verified stable and secure.
Destination to VCU Health Systems confirmed.
Final check for transport loading.
Patient loaded safely with no complications observed.
All transport protocols executed successfully.
Patient is en route to VCU Health Systems as designated.
Backboard fixation straps verified and secure.
Patient placed in transport position safely.
Patient's condition remains stable during transport.
All monitoring and support systems confirmed active.
Estimated time of arrival at VCU will be communicated upon stabilization.
Spinal precautions and medical status maintained and updated during en route.
Final confirmation of vitals for arrival documentation.
Protocol 3-15 and IV status reported to transport team.
Altered mental status and GCS score included in handoff.
No medications administered during the transport.
